title: State and Memory
description: The Memory interface saves and restores deployment state between runs.
why_persistence_body: CReact apps describe infrastructure and processes. When the app stops and restarts, you need to know what was already deployed. Otherwise every restart re-creates everything from scratch. The Memory interface solves this by saving and restoring deployment state.
memory_interface_intro: render() requires a Memory implementation. You implement two methods: getState and saveState. CReact does not ship a built-in implementation. You choose the storage backend.
file_memory_intro: The simplest implementation persists state as JSON files on disk:
tip_stack_name: The third argument to render() is the stack name. It identifies your app's state file. Different stack names allow multiple independent state stores.
use_async_output_intro: useAsyncOutput creates resources whose outputs CReact persists. On restart, the handler receives saved outputs via setOutputs(prev => ...).
what_gets_persisted_body: Only values passed to setOutputs() are persisted. Regular signals, effects, and local variables are not saved. They reinitialize on each run.
reconciliation_body: When the app restarts, CReact compares the new component tree against the saved state. Outputs are hydrated from the saved state, but handlers re-run for all nodes to re-establish side effects (intervals, subscriptions, etc.). Handlers must be idempotent. They receive previously saved outputs and can skip work that's already done.
info_gitignore: Add the state directory (e.g. .state/) to your .gitignore.
memory_system_link: For the full Memory interface including optional locking, audit logging, and the DeploymentState structure, see Architecture: Memory System.
